questions.question: Explain the concept of Earned Value Management (EVM) and derive the formulas for Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI).
keyPoints: EVM integrates cost, schedule, and scope measurements
Interpretation: CPI/SPI < 1 unfavorable, > 1 favorable, = 1 on target

questions.question: Describe the eight quality characteristics defined in ISO/IEC 25010 standard for software product quality evaluation.

A software project has: Budget at Completion (BAC) = $400,000, Actual Cost (AC) = $250,000, Earned Value (EV) = $200,000, Planned Value (PV) = $220,000. Calculate: (i) Cost Variance (CV), (ii) Schedule Variance (SV), (iii) CPI, (iv) SPI, and (v) EAC.
What is the purpose of a Post-Implementation Review (PIR)? List the key activities involved in conducting a PIR.
keyPoints: Document lessons learned and best practices
Identify areas for improvement in future projects
Archive project documentation for organizational knowledge

questions.question: Explain the Weighted Scoring Model approach for evaluating software projects. Illustrate with a numerical example involving at least four evaluation criteria.
keyPoints: Define evaluation criteria relevant to project goals
Assign weights based on strategic importance (weights sum to 1)
Score each criterion on a defined scale
Calculate weighted sum: Total = Σ(weight × score)

questions.question: Describe the concept of Project Health Check. How does traffic light signaling help in communicating project status to stakeholders?
keyPoints: Three dimensions: Cost, Schedule, Quality (or Scope)

questions.question: A mid-sized software company is evaluating three competing project proposals: Project Alpha (web portal), Project Beta (mobile app), and Project Gamma (legacy system modernization). Each project requires different investment levels and has varying risk profiles. Propose a comprehensive evaluation framework that the company should use to select the most appropriate project. Include specific criteria, weighting methodology, and justify your approach.
keyPoints: Define evaluation criteria: Strategic fit, ROI, Technical feasibility, Market potential, Resource availability, Risk level
Use weighted scoring model: Assign weights based on organizational priorities
Consider both quantitative (NPV, ROI, payback) and qualitative factors
Sensitivity analysis to test weight changes
Recommendation based on highest weighted score considering risk tolerance

questions.question: You are appointed as the project manager for a software development project that is significantly over budget and behind schedule. The client has requested a detailed status report. Using Earned Value Management techniques, explain how you would: (a) Diagnose the current project health, (b) Forecast the final project cost, (c) Propose corrective actions to get the project back on track.
keyPoints: (a) Diagnosis: Calculate CPI, SPI, CV, SV; Identify magnitude of variances; Determine if variances are acceptable or critical; Compare against thresholds
(b) Forecasting: Use EAC = BAC/CPI (assuming current performance); Or EAC = AC + ETC (bottom-up estimate); Or EAC = BAC (if current variance is atypical)

(c) Corrective Actions: Fast-tracking (parallel activities); Crashing (adding resources); Scope reduction; Re-baseline if variances are permanent; Change request if scope expansion caused issues; Risk mitigation for remaining work

questions.question: Discuss the importance of linking project evaluation to the project management life cycle. How should evaluation be conducted at different phases: Initiation, Planning, Execution, Monitoring & Control, and Closure? Explain with specific metrics and techniques appropriate for each phase.
keyPoints: Initiation: Business case alignment, stakeholder satisfaction, feasibility metrics
Planning: Baseline establishment, estimation accuracy, risk assessment quality
Execution: Progress tracking, quality metrics, resource utilization, EVM metrics
Monitoring & Control: Variance analysis, trend analysis, CPI/SPI health indicators
Closure: Objective achievement, benefits realization, lessons learned, PIR
